2023 · NeurIPS 2023 · Agents

Chameleon: Plug-and-Play Compositional Reasoning with Large Language Models

An LLM planner writes a short natural-language "program" — a sequence of tool names like Text_Detector → Knowledge_Retrieval → Solution_Generator → Answer_Generator — over a fixed inventory of 13 heterogeneous tools (other LLM calls, vision models, Bing search, Python execution, rule-based modules), then executes it step by step, each module updating a shared cache for the next. Powered by GPT-4, Chameleon reaches 86.54% accuracy on ScienceQA and 98.78% on TabMWP, both new state of the art, without any tool-specific training.

Pan Lu, Baolin Peng, Hao Cheng, Michel Galley, Kai-Wei Chang, Ying Nian Wu, Song-Chun Zhu, Jianfeng Gao · UCLA & Microsoft Research
venue NeurIPS 2023 citations 482 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By early 2023, LLMs like GPT-3, PaLM, LLaMA, and ChatGPT had shown strong in-context learning and chain-of-thought reasoning, but the paper points at three concrete gaps that reasoning alone can't close: LLMs can't access up-to-date information stored on the web or in task-specific knowledge bases, can't reliably perform precise mathematical or logical computation, and can't call out to specialized models (e.g. an OCR or vision system) for capabilities they were never trained to have. A first wave of tool-augmented LLMs had started to address individual gaps — but the paper's read of that line of work is that each system is "either limited to a small number of tools or relying on domain-specific tools," so none of them generalizes across query types without being rebuilt per domain.

The motivating examples make the gap concrete. Answering "Which is the main persuasive appeal used in this ad?" requires decoding embedded text in an image, retrieving background knowledge about what ethos/pathos/logos even mean, and only then reasoning to an answer — three different capabilities chained together. Answering "Which animal's skin is adapted for survival in cold places?" instead needs an image caption plus a web search for domain terminology — a different chain, over the same underlying LLM. A system limited to a fixed small toolset, or one hand-wired for a single domain, cannot serve both queries without redesign.

// how it works

The method

Chameleon's core idea is to treat tool composition itself as a generation problem: an LLM-based planner, prompted with natural-language descriptions and usage examples of every available module, synthesizes a program — an ordered list of module names — to execute for a given query, rather than following a hand-authored decision tree or a domain-specific language. The module inventory M spans five categories: LLM-backed modules from OpenAI (Knowledge Retrieval, Query Generator, Row/Column Lookup, Table Verbalizer, Program Generator, Solution Generator), a Hugging Face Image Captioner, a GitHub-sourced Text Detector, Bing Search, Python execution (Program Verifier, Program Executor), and a rule-based Answer Generator. Because the generated program is a sequence of plain module names rather than code in a domain-specific language, the authors argue it is easier for a non-expert to read, debug, and extend with new modules.

Execution is sequential and stateful: each module reads the current query plus a shared cache of intermediate results, does its job, and then either updates the query (update_input, e.g. Row Lookup replacing a large table with just the relevant rows) or writes a new result into the cache (update_cache, e.g. an image caption or a retrieved knowledge snippet) for downstream modules to read. To keep planning tractable, the search space is constrained per task: ScienceQA programs draw from a subset of the inventory suited to multi-modal QA (image captioning, text detection, knowledge retrieval, search), while TabMWP programs draw from a subset suited to tabular math (row/column lookup, table verbalization, program generation/verification/execution) — invalid programs fall back to fixed default sequences (e.g. Solution Generator → Answer Generator) modeled on the chain-of-thought and program-of-thought prompting baselines they compare against.

// what they found

The results

On ScienceQA (multi-modal scientific QA), Chameleon powered by GPT-4 reaches 86.54% overall test accuracy, beating GPT-4 with plain chain-of-thought prompting (83.99%) and improving on the best previously published few-shot result, GPT-3 CoT at 75.17%, by +11.37 points. The ChatGPT-powered version reaches 79.93%, itself ahead of ChatGPT CoT's 78.31%. On TabMWP (tabular math reasoning), Chameleon with GPT-4 reaches 98.78%, versus 96.93% for GPT-4 with program-of-thought prompting and 90.81% for GPT-4 CoT; relative to the best prior published result, Codex PoT-SC at 81.8%, that is a +17.0-point jump, and +7.97 over GPT-4 CoT specifically. Neither benchmark required any Chameleon-specific fine-tuning — every gain comes from the choice and ordering of tools per query.

ModelScienceQATabMWP
GPT-3 CoT (best prior published, few-shot)75.17—
Codex PoT-SC (best prior published, few-shot)—81.8
ChatGPT CoT78.3182.03
Chameleon (ChatGPT)79.9393.28
GPT-4 CoT83.9990.81
GPT-4 PoT—96.93
Chameleon (GPT-4)86.5498.78

The paper's own ablation-style analysis is arguably its most interesting result: swapping the planner LLM from ChatGPT to GPT-4 (identical tool inventory, identical prompts) changes not just accuracy but behavior. GPT-4 generates fewer distinct programs than ChatGPT on both tasks (11 vs. 14 on ScienceQA, 19 vs. 28 on TabMWP) — read by the authors as more consistent tool selection — and calls tools more selectively: on ScienceQA, GPT-4's planner calls Knowledge Retrieval 81% of the time versus ChatGPT's 72%, but calls the Text Detector only 30% of the time versus ChatGPT's 17%, suggesting a better sense of when text-in-image extraction is actually needed rather than calling it reflexively.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The headline comparisons are apples-to-apples in the ways that matter most: Chameleon (GPT-4) is compared against GPT-4 CoT and GPT-4 PoT using the same underlying model, and Chameleon (ChatGPT) against ChatGPT CoT/PoT — so the gain is attributable to tool composition rather than a bigger base model. But the "plug-and-play" framing in the title is softer than it first appears once you read the implementation details: the program search space is manually constrained per task — ScienceQA programs are restricted to a hand-picked subset of the 13-tool inventory, TabMWP to a different hand-picked subset — and non-compliant programs silently fall back to fixed default sequences modeled directly on the CoT/PoT baselines. That is a reasonable engineering choice for making planning tractable, but it means the LLM planner is choosing among a curated menu the authors already narrowed for each domain, not freely composing over the full 13-tool inventory the "plug-and-play" language implies.

Both evaluation tasks are also narrow by design: ScienceQA is multiple-choice and TabMWP's answers are short numeric/text strings, so both are cheap to grade automatically — but that also means Chameleon has only been demonstrated on tasks where a program either gets a discrete answer right or wrong, not on open-ended generation where tool selection errors might be harder to detect and score. The authors' own error analysis (Figure 6) surfaces a genuinely double-edged finding: on their 50-example ChatGPT-mistake sample, Chameleon (ChatGPT) introduces 13 new "tool planning" mistakes where the baseline ChatGPT had zero — i.e., composing tools is itself a new failure surface, not a pure win, even as it slashes image- and knowledge-category errors (32→10 and 37→6 respectively). The GPT-4 version reduces tool-planning errors to 1, suggesting this failure mode is largely a weaker-planner problem rather than fundamental to the approach, but it is real evidence against a naive "more tools, strictly better" reading.

The authors' own Limitations section (Appendix B) is candid about the rest: Chameleon "generates the program at one step, without incorporating a re-planning mechanism as the modules in the program are processed" — unlike DEPS-style interactive planners, if step 2 of a 4-step program fails or returns something unexpected, there is no built-in mechanism to revise steps 3–4 in light of that. The paper also flags that the design assumes the full list of module descriptions fits in the LLM's context window, which will not scale indefinitely as the tool inventory grows, and evaluates on exactly two benchmarks — both benefit from the same institution's own prior ScienceQA and TabMWP releases, so generalization to less curated, noisier real-world queries is asserted rather than demonstrated.

baselines

Fair and current for their moment — CoT and PoT prompting on the same base LLM (ChatGPT or GPT-4), which isolates the tool-composition contribution — but the comparison set is entirely prompting-based; no comparison to other 2023 tool-use agents (e.g. Toolformer, ReAct) on the same two benchmarks.

contamination

Not directly addressed; ScienceQA and TabMWP both predate GPT-4's training cutoff and are widely used public benchmarks, so some memorization risk exists for the underlying LLM regardless of Chameleon's tool layer — the paper does not test for it.

what's missed

The per-task hand-curated tool subset softens the "plug-and-play" claim; the two eval tasks are both auto-gradable multiple-choice/short-answer, leaving open how well planning generalizes to open-ended queries; and the 13-new-tool-planning-mistakes finding shows composition adds its own error surface.

would raise confidence

A held-out task the tool subset was not hand-tuned for, a comparison against contemporaneous tool-use agents on the same benchmarks, and a stated plan for what happens as the module inventory grows past what fits in context.

// bottom line

Chameleon's central bet — let an LLM synthesize which tools to call and in what order, rather than hard-coding a pipeline — pays off cleanly on two auto-graded benchmarks, with real, same-base-model-controlled gains (+11.37 on ScienceQA, +17.0 on TabMWP over the best prior published results). The honest caveats are in the paper's own appendix: the tool search space is hand-curated per task, there is no re-planning if an early module misfires, and the authors' own error analysis shows composition itself introduces new "tool planning" mistakes with a weaker planner — a nuance the headline accuracy numbers don't surface.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Letting an LLM planner synthesize a natural-language "program" over a diverse tool inventory — rather than hand-coding which tools fire when — set a new state of the art on two multi-modal reasoning benchmarks: 86.54% on ScienceQA (+11.37 over the best prior published few-shot result) and 98.78% on TabMWP (+17.0 over the best prior published result), with GPT-4 as the planner.
  2. The planner LLM's own quality matters beyond raw accuracy: swapping ChatGPT for GPT-4 (same tools, same prompts) made the generated programs more consistent (fewer distinct programs) and cut new "tool planning" errors from 13 down to 1 out of 50 sampled mistakes.
  3. "Plug-and-play" is aspirational more than literal in this implementation — the tool search space is manually narrowed per benchmark, and there is no re-planning step if an early module in the program returns something the rest of the pipeline didn't expect.
